key_concepts: permanent tissues (cardiac muscle, skeletal muscle, nerve)
covered: permanent tissues (cardiac muscle, skeletal muscle, nerve)

judge_scores.justification: Tests two distinct facts: homeostasis maintenance and the response to stress changes.
Tests multiple facts: definition of hypertrophy, mechanism (cell size), and three specific processes involved.
Tests two separate facts: hyperplasia definition and the source of new cells.
Tests knowledge of three tissue types as a single fact about hypertrophy-only tissues.
Requires understanding that cardiac muscle is a 'permanent tissue' which isn't explained in the card.
Perfectly atomic card testing a single exception to hyperplasia-cancer progression.
Tests two related but distinct facts: progression to dysplasia and progression to cancer.
Tests multiple facts: definition of atrophy, its cause, and two mechanisms of size reduction.
Perfectly atomic card testing only the mechanism of cell number decrease in atrophy.
Tests two mechanisms simultaneously rather than focusing on one.
Tests both the definition of metaplasia and the most common tissue type affected.
Tests three facts: the specific cell types involved and the causative factor.
Perfectly atomic card testing only the reversibility of metaplasia.
Tests progression to both dysplasia and cancer as related but distinct outcomes.
Tests both the definition of dysplasia and its association with precancerous cells.
Tests two causes of dysplasia rather than a single fact.
Perfectly atomic card testing only when dysplasia becomes irreversible.
Tests two definitions in one card rather than focusing on a single concept.
Tests both what CIN represents and its relationship to cancer.
Perfectly atomic card testing only the reversibility condition of dysplasia.
Perfectly atomic card testing only the definition of aplasia.
Perfectly atomic card testing only the definition of hypoplasia.
Tests both when injury occurs and the relationship between stress and adaptation.
Perfectly atomic card testing only neuron susceptibility to ischemia.
Perfectly atomic card testing only skeletal muscle resistance to ischemia.
Perfect card - tests single mechanism clearly and accurately.
Excellent basic definition card with no weaknesses.
Tests two facts: definition of hypoxemia AND specific PaO2 cutoff value.
Tests both the PaO2 normalcy AND the mechanism, slightly non-atomic.
Perfect single-fact card about a distinctive clinical finding.
Tests two separate facts: oxidation state AND functional consequence.
Tests two distinct clinical findings rather than one atomic fact.
Excellent single-fact card about key pathologic distinction.
Perfect counterpart to card 7, testing single key feature.
Clear cause-and-effect relationship tested as single fact.
Parallel to card 9, testing single mechanism clearly.
Single consequence of membrane damage clearly stated.
Specific molecular mechanism tested atomically.
Tests three separate terms with their definitions in one card.
Good complement to card 13, testing just the list of three terms.
Tests both group size AND inflammation status as defining features.
Tests three facts: ATP-dependence, single cells, and no inflammation.
Parenthetical comparison to necrosis might require knowledge of necrosis's nature.
Tests four separate characteristics in one card.
Single association between condition and necrosis type.
Tests both appearance AND mechanism in one card.
Lists three different organs rather than testing one fact.
Tests two descriptive features of appearance.
Perfect single association between TB and caseous necrosis.
Tests appearance, cause (calcium), AND mechanism (saponification) in one card.
Perfect card testing a single important pathology fact.
Excellent atomic card about a key pathology concept.
Tests two facts (affected tissue and appearance) when it should focus on one.
Tests two conditions rather than one, slightly reducing atomicity.
Tests two separate mechanisms (proteases and endonucleases) in one card.
Perfectly atomic card testing a single mechanism.
Tests two triggers rather than focusing on one.
Tests two characteristics (shrinkage and intact membrane) rather than one.
Excellent atomic card about a specific enzyme function.
Perfect companion card to index 8, testing single fact.
Tests two related facts (Bcl2 inactivation and cytochrome c leak) in sequence.
Tests ligand-receptor binding and outcome, slightly reducing atomicity.
Tests two separate mechanisms (perforins and granzymes) in one answer.
Tests three facts: definition, and two types of damage mechanisms.
Tests enzyme, substrate, and product in one card rather than focusing on one relationship.
Perfect atomic card about a single pathophysiology concept.
Tests both structural configuration and staining property in one card.
Excellent atomic card about a specific histological finding.
Perfect card linking one amyloid type to its cause.
Excellent companion to card 18, maintaining atomicity.
Tests three separate clinical manifestations in one card, violating atomicity principle.
